title-group.article-title: Understanding the epidemiological trends and economic impact of dengue in Indonesia: A systematic literature review

contrib-group.aff: 1Eijkman Research Center for Molecular Biology, National Research and Innovation Agency, Jakarta, Indonesia
4Department of Child Health, Faculty of Medicine, Universitas Indonesia – Dr. Cipto Mangunkusumo General Hospital, Jakarta, Indonesia

fn.p: Elaine Gallagher was an employee of Takeda Pharmaceuticals at the time that this review was conducted and is currently employed at Bayer, Zurich, Switzerland.
Fauchil Wardati was Takeda Innovative Medicines employee and now currently Doctoral Program of Immunology at The University Medical Center Groningen, Netherlands.

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 Takeda Pharmaceuticals International AG, Zurich, Switzerland

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

list-item.p: Dengue cases in Indonesia have increased since 2000, with periodic peaks.
Bali and East Kalimantan have had the highest regional incidence.
Dengue cases were typically highest in 5–14-year-olds, followed by 15–44-year-olds.
Underreporting of dengue in Indonesia was widespread.
Severe dengue cases imposed a significant societal and economic burden in the country.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: To identify and analyze evidence on the epidemiological trends and economic impact of dengue in Indonesia.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Electronic databases (PubMed, Embase, and the Cochrane Database) and gray literature were searched for publicly available data on the epidemiological (2000-2020) and economic (2010-2020) dengue burden in Indonesia. Data from the selected studies were extracted and summarized descriptively. An additional search conducted identified country-level dengue studies in Indonesia published in recent years (2021-2024).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: For epidemiological burden, 143 publications (94 published studies; 49 gray literature articles) were included. The number of reported dengue cases increased, ranging from 21,143 in 2000 to 204,172 in 2016. Incidence rates (IRs) peaked at 78.9 cases per 100,000 population in 2016. Between 2017 and 2020, dengue cases were generally highest among 5–14-year-olds. While the number of deaths has fluctuated since 2000, case fatality rates have declined. Regionally, Bali and East Kalimantan reported some of the highest IRs between 2000 and 2020. Underreporting was widespread, with expansion factors ranging from 1.66 for hospitalized cases to 34.01 for ambulatory cases. Results from eight studies showed that dengue was associated with a significant economic burden, with higher costs for hospitalized/fatal cases than for ambulatory cases. The additional search identified 11 relevant articles; however, none provided country-level primary data on dengue incidence, mortality, serotype distribution, or economic outcomes in Indonesia.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Dengue burden in Indonesia is high, with severe cases associated with a significant economic burden.
PROSPERO Registration Number: CRD42021266804

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Dengue, a viral infection mainly transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, is one of the most rapidly increasing vector-borne diseases worldwide [1]. Dengue virus (DENV), of which four serotypes exist, causes mostly asymptomatic or mild-to-moderate infections; however, ∼5% of patients develop severe, life-threatening disease [2].

An estimated 100-400 million dengue infections occur annually, and 70% of the global disease burden is found in Asia [1]. The first dengue case in Indonesia was reported in 1968; subsequently, there has been a 700–800-fold increase in the annual incidence of dengue over 45 years [3]. However, with improved case management, fatality rates have decreased from 41.0% to 0.73% [3]. The burden of dengue is challenging to compare across countries because of differences in how case data are reported. For instance, in Indonesia, underreporting of dengue is common, and routine disease surveillance is largely passive [4]. In 2019, among 11 Southeast Asian countries, Indonesia was ranked eighth for the age-standardized incidence rate (IR; 1037.02 per 100,000 population) but was first for disease burden (age-standardized disability-adjusted life years rate: 290.10 per 100,000 population) [5]. In 2020, Indonesia ranked eighth among Southeast Asian countries in terms of dengue IR, but second after Vietnam, based on the reported case numbers [6].

The global economic and societal costs of dengue are estimated at ∼US$ 39 billion annually, with Southeast Asia contributing US$ 15.8 billion [7]. In Indonesia, there is large variability among cost-of-illness studies in terms of the methodologies used, treatment settings, and reporting of costs. However, evidence indicates that costs are substantial with the aggregate economic cost of dengue in Indonesia as high as US$ 738.27 (adjusted to 2020 US$) million in 2017 [8].

Dengue incidence worldwide is expected to rise in the coming years because of increased urbanization, population growth in endemic regions, and the continued geographical expansion of Aedes mosquitoes in response to climate change [9]. Monitoring dengue infection trends over time is a key component of disease prevention, as up-to-date estimates of its epidemiological and economic impact may increase disease awareness, trigger improvement in surveillance, and inform the effective allocation of health resources and prioritization by the government. Therefore, this systematic literature review was conducted to identify and analyze existing evidence to better understand the epidemiological trends and economic impact of dengue in Indonesia over time.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This review (PROSPERO registration number: CRD42021266804) was conducted in accordance with the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews and the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses [10,11].

sec.title: Data sources and search strategy
sec.p: PubMed, Embase, the Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, Cochrane Central, and the Database of Abstracts of Reviews of Effects were searched on 2 June 2021 for epidemiology and cost studies conducted during 2000-2020 and 2010-2020, respectively. Pre-2010 cost studies were excluded because the data may not be based on current dengue prevention and control strategies. Bibliographies of selected articles were searched for relevant studies. A targeted search was conducted on gray literature websites from government and public health agencies and thesis catalogs of the main universities in Indonesia (Supplementary Table 1). All retrieved articles were screened against predefined eligibility criteria (Supplementary Table 2). Articles in English or Indonesian were included if they reported epidemiological outcomes, such as IR or number of cases of dengue, case definition (Supplementary Table 3), seroprevalence, serotype distribution, disease severity, case fatality rates (CFRs), expansion factors (EFs), seasonality, and economic outcomes. EFs are multiplication or adjustment factors used to correct the underreporting of dengue cases in official passive surveillance systems. The searches included both medical subject headings (MeSH and Emtree) and free-text terms (Supplementary Table 4).

sec.title: Data extraction and synthesis
sec.p: In phase I, the titles and abstracts of the retrieved articles were independently screened by two reviewers against the eligibility criteria, with discrepancies resolved by a third reviewer. In phase II, the full texts of all articles selected in phase I were further assessed for eligibility by a reviewer using the same eligibility criteria. Data were extracted into predefined data extraction forms by a reviewer, and a random 10% sample was validated by a second reviewer; discrepancies were resolved by a third reviewer.

Risk-of-bias assessments were used to determine the quality of all epidemiological and cost studies included. A single reviewer conducted both data extraction and risk-of-bias assessment. All extracted data were summarized descriptively. For studies based on the same cohort, all publications were included if they reported different results. If the same results were reported, the most recent publication was cited.
For economic data, where possible, costs were converted to 2020 US$ using the Indonesian consumer price index (CPI).

sec.title: Updated literature search
sec.p: During the publication process of this manuscript, an additional literature search was conducted on the topic of Indonesia and dengue, covering the two focus areas of epidemiology and economics, for studies published between 1 January 2021 to 4 November 2025. The purpose of this additional search was to identify any recent country-level studies or new insights of dengue in Indonesia during the last 4 years. The data sources and search strategy were performed consistently using ProQuest Dialog and the databases MEDLINE, Embase, BIOSIS, the Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, Cochrane Central, International Pharmaceutical Abstracts, and Northern Light Life Sciences Conference Abstracts.

sec.title: Epidemiology
sec.p:
Overall, 143 publications were identified—94 published studies (84 full texts; 10 abstracts) and 49 articles from gray literature (Figure 1, Supplementary Tables 5 and 6, and Supplementary Appendix). All 84 studies were rated as good (>70% score) according to the risk-of-bias assessment (Supplementary Table 7).Figure 1
PRISMA diagram. D, duplicates; DARE, Database of Abstracts of Reviews of Effects; D/PD, data/publication date limit; FNF, full-text not found; L, language; O, outcome; P, population; PRISMA, Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses; S, study design.Figure 1 dummy alt text

sec.title: Dengue incidence
sec.p:
According to the Indonesian Ministry of Health (MoH), the total number of dengue hemorrhagic fever (DHF) cases and IRs increased during 2000-2020, with fluctuating peaks (Figure 2a) [[12], [13], [14]]. Cases were lowest in 2000 at 21,134, increased until 2007, and stabilized during 2009-2010 at approximately 156,000. Subsequently, peaks were registered in 2016 (204,172 cases) and 2019 (138,131 cases). Multiple publications reported DHF incidence data, but across disparate periods (Supplementary Table 8). Similar to MoH reports showing increasing incidence, a study analyzing data from DengueNet and World Health Organization sources found that the IR increased from 23.2 to 43 per 100,000 population during 1980-2010 and 2000-2010, respectively [15]. Overall, data from surveillance sources and published studies suggest that dengue incidence is increasing with time and exhibits cyclical peaks.Figure 2
(a) Number of cases and incidence rate of DHF in Indonesia; (b) Total number of deaths and case fatality rate for overall DHF in Indonesia.
*Number of DHF deaths for years 2000–2006 were not available and were back-calculated using the number of DHF cases and the CFR for these years.
DHF, dengue hemorrhagic fever.Figure 2 dummy alt textSources: [[12], [13], [14]].

National surveillance on DHF incidence by age group was available for the period 2017-2020. Cases generally dropped after 2017 and then increased over the next 3 years, except among 5–14- and 15–44-year olds [12]. However, the highest proportion of cases was observed mostly among 5–14-year-olds, followed by 15–44-years-olds (Supplementary Table 9). Among publications, incidence by age group was reported from the placebo arm of a dengue vaccine (CYD14) trial conducted from June 2011 to December 2013 in children aged 2-14 years in five Southeast Asian countries, including 623 participants from Indonesia [16]. The trial captured dengue IRs after active surveillance and virological confirmation. IRs for virologically confirmed dengue (VCD) were greater in younger children (5.6 per 100 person-years in 5–8-year-olds vs 1.2 per 100 person-years in 13–16-year-olds). Additionally, the overall VCD incidence that was initially clinically diagnosed by a trial investigator was 1825 cases per 100,000 person-years [17]. However, the VCD (irrespective of clinical diagnosis) incidence was much higher at 3017 cases per 100,000 person-years, suggesting that much of the burden is undiagnosed. Indeed, only ∼29% of VCD cases in the clinical trial were diagnosed by investigators as dengue. Nealon et al. [17] reported that their data were considerably higher than the average IRs from national surveillance systems (262.9 cases per 100,000 person-years) and that active surveillance systems captured many more symptomatic dengue cases than did routine passive surveillance.

sec.title: Disease severity
sec.p: From 2000-2020, the total number of deaths from DHF in Indonesia fluctuated with no clear pattern, with the lowest number of deaths registered in 2018 (467) and the highest in 2007 (1599; Figure 2b) [[12], [13], [14]]. In contrast, the CFRs showed a decreasing trend throughout this period, ranging from 2.0% in 2000 to 0.69% in 2020. Based on MoH reports on DHF deaths by age group [12], available for the period 2017-2020, the highest percentages were consistently reported in 5–14-year-olds, followed by 15–44- and 1–4-year-olds; however, DHF deaths among 5–14- and 15–44-year-olds dropped to their lowest in 2020 (Supplementary Table 10).

No national surveillance data were available on dengue hospitalizations. Most publications also failed to report dengue hospitalization rates in the general population (Supplementary Table 11 and Supplementary Appendix).

sec.title: Dengue incidence
sec.p: At the time this review was conducted, Indonesia was divided into 34 administrative provinces (Supplementary Figure 1). MoH data revealed that regional DHF IRs have increased since 2000 for most provinces, with Bali and East Kalimantan almost consistently reporting the highest IRs [[12], [13], [14]]. Since data became available for North Kalimantan in 2014, IRs have generally been high, with some fluctuations in 2017 and 2018. Jakarta reported some of the highest IRs from 2000, with a downward trend since 2010. Consistent with national incidence data, most regions reported the largest dengue outbreak in 2016, with regions such as Jakarta, Yogyakarta, and Southeast Sulawesi, alongside Bali, East Kalimantan, and North Kalimantan, reporting IRs above 100 cases per 100,000 population (Supplementary Figure 2). Papua, West Papua, and Maluku consistently reported the lowest DHF incidences.

Only a few publications have reported on the overall incidence of dengue in specific regions, and comparisons across studies and with MoH data are challenging owing to the different periods analyzed and criteria for dengue case classification (Supplementary Table 8). However, publications reporting the age distribution of dengue cases at the regional level generally showed a higher proportion of dengue cases among younger age groups. For instance, in Surabaya and Jember in East Java, Semarang in Central Java, and Samarinda and Balikpapan in East Kalimantan, 0–10-year-olds reported the highest dengue cases, followed by 11–20-year-olds (Supplementary Table 12). In Jakarta, 11–20-year-olds, followed by 21–30-year-olds, registered a higher dengue case load than 0–10-year-olds.

sec.title: Case definition
sec.p: While dengue incidence by multiple case definitions was not available at the national level, regional data, mostly on dengue fever (DF) and DHF, were reported in publications (Supplementary Table 8). Heterogeneity in methodology, age groups analyzed, data periods, and reporting of results hinder comparisons of incidence across different case definitions. Karyanti et al. [18] analyzed the characteristics of patients with dengue admitted to a hospital in Jakarta between 2009 and 2013. Overall, dengue shock syndrome (DSS) was more frequent in 5–9-year-olds (38.4% of all DSS cases), while DHF and DF were more frequent among 10–14-year-olds (37.3% and 36.2% of DHF and DF cases, respectively). This pattern was consistent with data from seven major hospitals in Bandung, West Java, in 2015, where DSS was mostly found in children aged ≤15 years (70.5% of cases vs 29.5% of cases in those aged >15 years, P < 0.001) [19]. Temporal patterns of DHF were reported by Harapan et al. [20] using provincial IR mapping of DHF in Indonesia between 2011 and 2016 (Supplementary Figure 3). IRs increased throughout the period across different provinces, with Kalimantan becoming increasingly affected over time. West Java contributed the highest average number of DHF cases each year, and Bali reported the highest IRs.

sec.title: Disease severity
sec.p: The MoH reported CFRs for DHF at the regional level for 2000-2020 (Supplementary Figure 4). Provinces such as Bali and East Kalimantan, which had some of the highest IRs, reported the lowest CFRs, while Maluku, which had the lowest DHF incidence, reported the highest CFRs across most years since 2010. The total number of deaths due to DHF by region was sporadically reported across years, and no trends could be identified.

Data from publications were largely consistent with the MoH data. Harapan et al. [20] showed that between 2011 and 2016, several provinces where dengue is not endemic experienced dengue outbreaks associated with high CFRs, such as the epidemics of 2012 and 2015 in West Papua and the 2013 Papua outbreak (Supplementary Figure 5). Although Bali reported the highest IRs, the CFR was consistently <1%. Refer to the Supplementary Appendix for additional details.

sec.title: Seasonality
sec.p: In six districts of North Sumatra, the number of DHF cases annually between 2003 and 2011 followed a cyclical pattern with seasonal variation, peaking between July and December [21] (Supplementary Table 13). In Sumatra and Kalimantan, from 2006 to 2016, the incidence of DF peaked around January each year [22]. The authors surmised that the seasonal pattern of DF in these regions was closely related to minimum temperature and relative humidity, with the highest peaks corresponding to El Niño/La Niña events that caused longer dry or rainy seasons. In Cirebon, West Java, from January 2011 to December 2017, Astuti et al. [23] reported that the number of notified cases during the rainy season (November-May) was two-fold higher than that during the dry season (June-October). The highest dengue notification peaks consistently occurred in January and February. In Bali, DHF cases from 2001 to 2010 were highest in the first half of each year, peaking between January and February and July [24]. The same study showed that the peak DHF months differed across provinces. In 2009 and 2010, cases peaked around January in East Java, April-May in Jakarta, and May-June in Bali.

Collectively, these studies revealed that dengue cases are reported throughout the year in Indonesia with fluctuating incidence; the peak months varied across regions, showing seasonal patterns, with temperature, humidity, and rainfall playing a role.

sec.title: Seroprevalence
sec.p: National and international surveillance sources did not report data on dengue seroprevalence in Indonesia. In articles, at both regional and nationwide levels, seroprevalence for all age groups, including children and adults, was rarely reported, and most studies did not stratify results by age group (Supplementary Table 14); however, seroprevalence appeared to increase with age, with high transmission in children. Refer to the Supplementary Appendix for additional details on the increase in seroprevalence with age.

sec.title: Serotype
sec.p: National and international surveillance data were not current. However, several publications reported dengue serotype distribution in Indonesia, although data at the nationwide level were rarely reported, and a trend could not be established owing to limited reporting of yearly and age-related distributions (Supplementary Table 15). Data from the CYD14 trial [16] showed that among 2–14-year-olds, there was a shift from predominantly DENV-2 to DENV-1 during 2011 to 2013. A large (n = 776) nationwide study conducted on children in 2014 showed that DENV-2 serotype was the most frequent, followed by DENV-1 and DENV-3 serotypes, with some variation across the country [25]. Although less frequent, DENV-4 was also observed, particularly in Sumatra, Java, and Bali. Notably, DENV-3 was the predominant serotype detected during the 2016 epidemic [26,27]. Overall, the data suggest that the predominant dengue serotypes in Indonesia have alternated over the years. Since 2019, DENV-2 has been the predominant serotype, followed by DENV-1 (Supplementary Table 15).

sec.title: Expansion Factors
sec.p: Only three studies reported expansion factors (EFs) for dengue in Indonesia (Supplementary Table 16). The EFs ranged from 1.66 for hospitalized cases to 34.01 for ambulatory cases [4]. Nealon et al. [17] reported separate EFs for different case definitions: 9.4 for dengue clinically diagnosed by a study investigator, 11.5 for VCD (irrespective of clinical diagnosis), and 6.9 for clinically diagnosed VCD. Overall, the data indicate that the choice of EFs depends on the case definition and treatment setting.

sec.title: Economic impact of dengue
sec.p: A total of 106 dengue cost-related publications were identified from the literature searches, and eight publications fulfilled the eligibility criteria (Supplementary Figure 6, Supplementary Table 17, and Supplementary Appendix). Based on the risk-of-bias assessment, five studies were rated good (>70% score), while three were rated poor (<50% score; Supplementary Table 18).

Economic data from relevant studies were analyzed after converting costs to 2020 US$ using the Indonesian CPI. Costs associated with dengue outbreaks varied depending on location and treatment setting; however, the overall costs were substantial, varied over time, and were high for hospitalized and fatal cases (Supplementary Table 19). In 2010, for a hospitalized setting, the estimated average direct and indirect costs per case in Indonesia were US$ 364.86 and US$ 80.58, respectively, whereas for an ambulatory setting, costs were lower, at US$ 97.22 for direct and US$ 37.75 for indirect costs per case [28]. After adjusting for underreporting, the total annual estimated dengue costs for the period 2001-2010 amounted to US$ 497.93 million, of which US$ 144.02 million was direct cost and US$ 353.15 million was indirect cost. In 2013, the average direct cost per nonfatal dengue episode in Indonesia was US$ 69.85 and US$ 432.29 for ambulatory and hospitalized settings, respectively, whereas the indirect costs were US$ 42.17 and US$ 75.12 for the respective settings [29]. The cost per fatal episode was substantially greater, at US$ 131,794 and US$ 85,666 per child and adult death, respectively. The total annual aggregated cost was estimated at US $2892 million (consisting of US$ 1998.1 million direct and US$ 894.3 million indirect costs). Similar trends were reported by Wilastenegoro et al. [8]. In 2018, the weighted average cost per nonfatal dengue case was US$ 54.62 at the national level, while the overall average cost per case (including fatal cases) was US$ 97.98. Costs per fatal episode were estimated at US$ 115,138.56 per child and US$ 74,840.06 per adult case. The nationwide aggregated annual cost of dengue was reported as US$ 738.27 million, of which hospitalization at US$ 330.33 million, followed by fatal cases at US$ 326.82 million (US$ 151.66 million for children and US$ 175.16 million for adult), contributed the largest amounts [8]. The aggregated annual cost of ambulatory cases was US$ 41.84 million, whereas for non-medically attended cases it was US$ 39.28 million. At the city level (Yogyakarta city), the aggregated annual economic burden was US$ 0.17 million for ambulatory cases, US$ 1.34 million for hospitalized cases, and US$ 0.09 million for non-medically attended cases [8]. Nadjib et al. [30] estimated the direct and indirect costs of dengue for hospitalized and ambulatory cases in 2015. Costs at the nationwide level and for three provinces—Yogyakarta, Bali, and Jakarta—were reported. The combined total cost per patient per episode in each province was $115.9, $283.5, and $201.4 for outpatient cases and $775.1, $1,112.6, and $1205 for inpatient cases, respectively.
None of the identified studies reported data on productive days lost (school or workdays).

sec.title: Updated literature search
sec.p: The additional search aimed to identify country-level studies and new insights on dengue in Indonesia published in recent years (2021-2024). The additional literature search resulted in 582 publications. The results were filtered into articles published during 2000-2024. First, the titles and abstracts were screened to determine whether they met the original eligibility criteria. Of the 582 results, 11 articles were identified as potentially relevant considering our primary epidemiological outcomes of IR/number of cases of dengue, case definition, seroprevalence, serotype distribution, disease severity, CFRs, EFs, seasonality, and economic outcomes, as well as the objective for national and/or regional data (Supplementary Table 20). Upon full-text review of the 11 identified articles, none was found to provide country-level primary data on dengue incidence, mortality, serotype distribution, or economic outcomes in Indonesia.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our findings show that the burden of dengue is high in Indonesia, with IRs increasing since 2000. Cases peaked during 2007-2010, and subsequently, peaks were also observed in 2016 and 2019. Regional data showed a similar increasing trend with cyclical peaks. Bali and East Kalimantan were the regions with some of the highest reported IRs between 2000 and 2020, whereas Papua, West Papua, and Maluku had the lowest IRs. Different regional incidences may reflect not only differing geographical and climatic conditions across the different islands of Indonesia but also different population densities, urbanization trends, housing infrastructure, vector control measures, and reporting practices [4,22,31].

Studies have generally reported a higher incidence of dengue in children than in adults. Between 2017 and 2020, according to the MoH data, most dengue cases occurred in children aged 5-14 years, indicating that dengue in Indonesia is both a pediatric disease and a public health concern in adults. This is consistent with recently published data from Southeast Asia, where the highest age-standardized rate of dengue incidence was observed in 5–14-year-olds (1447.19 [95% uncertainty interval: 1263.18-1823.01]), followed by >70-year-olds (1356.32 [1222.49-1538.18]) [5]. Our findings show that after 5–14-year-olds, 15–44-year-olds had the highest proportion of dengue cases. This discrepancy with Tian et al. may be because in hyperendemic areas such as Indonesia, dengue is thought to be primarily a disease of older children and young adults, likely reflecting the timing of initial exposure during childhood [32].

IRs from published studies were greater than those reported by the MoH. This is likely because routine passive surveillance systems tend to capture only those dengue cases that are treated at healthcare facilities. In fact, the high number of dengue cases reported by Wahyono et al. [4] was calculated using an EF of 34.01 for ambulatory cases to account for underreporting. Furthermore, clinical trial (CYD14) data revealed that <30% of the laboratory-confirmed dengue cases in the trial were initially diagnosed as dengue by investigators [17], suggesting that current diagnostic practices may contribute to underreporting. Thus, studies that do not adjust for underreporting most likely underestimate the impact and costs of dengue.

Although dengue cases were reported year-round in Indonesia, fluctuating incidences revealed seasonal patterns. In Sumatra, Kalimantan, and West Java [22,23], cases peaked during the monsoon season. This is in accordance with the seasonal pattern of dengue in the country, where epidemics are usually associated with heavy rainfall [33]. However, the peak months can vary across regions. For instance, in Ambon, in the eastern province of Maluku, the dengue peak season occurred during June-November and not during the first half of the year, which is typically the dengue peak season in western provinces [33].

All four DENV serotypes currently cocirculate in Indonesia, but the predominant serotypes have alternated throughout the years and vary across regions. Recently published data support the presence of varying serotypes across regions. For instance, in early 2019, an outbreak of severe dengue was reported in Manado, North Sulawesi, with DENV-3 being the predominant serotype (75.9%) [34]. However, during an outbreak in 2019 in Malinau and Tarakan in North Kalimantan, DENV-2 was reported as the predominant serotype [35]. DENV-2 is the serotype most commonly associated with severe dengue [36], and regular monitoring of shifts in serotype prevalence at the national level may aid in predicting dengue outbreaks.

Despite the increase in incidence, CFRs in Indonesia have gradually decreased with time. Since 1980, the CFR has decreased by half each decade [20]. This was likely due to greater awareness and improved healthcare. More recent progress may be attributed to the National Health Insurance program known as Jaminan Kesehatan Nasional. Between 2014 and 2019, Jaminan Kesehatan Nasional coverage expanded to reach 83% of the population and reduced individual out-of-pocket expenditures on healthcare by ∼32% [37]. Additional preventive measures are under way, such as the National Strategic Plan for the Dengue Control Programme, launched in 2021 [38], which targets lowering IRs to below 49 per 100,000 population and CFR to below 0.5% by 2025 through various strategic initiatives.

Dengue in Indonesia has a high-cost burden. However, owing to differences in methodologies among cost-of-illness studies, no specific temporal pattern could be elucidated. Studies have consistently shown a greater proportion of the total costs associated with dengue hospitalization or death [8,29], although only a small fraction of the overall cases result in fatalities. As approximately half of the costs of nonfatal episodes are typically borne by the patient’s family and friends [8], prevention and early detection may substantially reduce the patient burden. Notably, the economic and societal burden of dengue is likely to be even higher when one considers productivity losses due to dengue, an area that we found to be insufficiently studied in Indonesia.

Prevention and control of dengue depends on effective vector control and active mosquito and virus surveillance, which are expensive and difficult to sustain. Increasing sustainable community engagement for dengue prevention has proved challenging, as knowledge levels and adherence to recommended practices remain poor in many urban localities [39,40]. The dramatic increase in dengue incidence suggests that current measures are insufficient for preventing dengue outbreaks worldwide. Additional preventive measures, such as vaccination, may help reduce dengue cases and corresponding costs, with several studies suggesting that a dengue vaccine would be cost-effective to introduce in a country with a high level of endemicity [41].

As the systematic literature review covered publications from 2000-2020, an additional literature search from 2021-2024 publications were conducted during the manuscript review process. The aim of the update was to identify any recent country-level studies or new insights on dengue in Indonesia during the last 4 years. After full-text review and careful assessment, none of the studies provided country-level primary data on dengue incidence, mortality, serotype distribution, or economic burden in Indonesia. Because the initial systematic literature review had already comprehensively captured evidence on dengue epidemiology and costing in Indonesia, the update was intentionally scoped to country-level studies to efficiently detect materially new insights without duplicating previously reviewed findings.

A limitation of this study is that data extraction was conducted by a single reviewer, and only 10% of the extracted data were quality checked. However, this was a comprehensive search of multiple sources, including gray literature and journal articles published in English and Indonesian, to minimize reporting bias. Results from this study not only highlight current trends in dengue epidemiology but also shed light on gaps in national surveillance data. The MoH did not report on dengue cases using more than one case definition; age distribution of dengue cases and deaths were reported sporadically; and national and international surveillance sources did not report on dengue hospitalization, seroprevalence, or serotype distribution. Such evidence gaps contribute to the underestimation of the disease burden, providing a strong case for improving national dengue surveillance.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Findings from this systematic literature review revealed rising cases of dengue in Indonesia over the past 20 years, with a high incidence in children as well as adults. Severe dengue cases impose a significant economic burden, wherein a relatively higher proportion of the total costs are associated with dengue hospitalization or death.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: Fauchil Wardati, Carolina Halim, Welly Welly, Randee Kastner, and Jing Shen are employees of Takeda Pharmaceuticals. Welly Welly and Jing Shen own Takeda stock. Elaine Gallagher was an employee of Takeda Pharmaceuticals at the time the systematic literature review was conducted. R. Tedjo Sasmono and Sri Rezeki Hadinegoro declare no conflicts of interest.

Surge of dengue virus infection and chikungunya fever in Bali in 2010: the burden of mosquito-borne infectious diseases in a tourist destination
Dengue virus serotype distribution based on serological evidence in pediatric urban population in Indonesia
Multiple introductions of dengue virus strains contribute to dengue outbreaks in East Kalimantan, Indonesia, in 2015-2016

ref.mixed-citation: Wijewickrama A., Fernando S., Bandara Jayerathne G.S., Perera P.A., Abeynaike S.A., Gomes L., et al. Emergence of a dengue virus serotype 2 causing the largest ever dengue epidemic in Sri Lanka. bioRxiv. 2018. 10.1101/329318 [accessed 10 June 2024].

element-citation.article-title: World Bank approves support to strengthen health insurance, improve quality of health care in Indonesia
Strategi Nasional Penanggulangan Dengue 2021–2025

element-citation.source: Int J Environ Res Public Health

element-citation.article-title: Knowledge, attitudes and practices toward dengue fever, vector control, and vaccine acceptance among the general population in countries from Latin America and Asia Pacific: a cross-sectional study (GEMKAP)
Economic evaluations of dengue vaccination in the southeast Asia region: evidence from a systematic review

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This study was funded by Takeda Pharmaceuticals International AG, Zurich. This support was funded by PT. Takeda Innovative Medicines, Indonesia. The sponsor was involved in the design of the systematic literature review and analyses of the results; and was given the opportunity to review the manuscript before submission. The authors retained full control of the manuscript content.

sec.title: Ethical approval statement
sec.p: Not required.

sec.title: Acknowledgments
sec.p: Egbe Ubamadu and Laura Mora of P95 Epidemiology and Pharmacovigilance, Leuven, Belgium, were involved in conducting the systematic literature review. Writing support for the development of this article was provided by Michelle Rebello, PhD, of Cactus Life Sciences (part of Cactus Communications). This support was funded by PT. Takeda Innovative Medicines, Indonesia.

sec.title: Author contributions
sec.p: Conceptualization and methodology: Elaine Gallagher, Randee Kastner, Jing Shen. Data collection: R. Tedjo Sasmono, Fauchil Wardati, Carolina Halim, Welly Welly, Jing Shen. Data analysis: All authors. Writing and reviewing: All authors.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: The datasets, including the redacted study protocol, redacted statistical analysis plan, and any individual participants’ data supporting the results reported in this article, will be made available within 3 months from initial request to researchers who provide a methodologically sound proposal. The data will be provided after de-identification, in compliance with applicable privacy laws, data protection regulations, and requirements for consent and anonymization.

fn.p: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ijregi.2026.100860.
